commonIssues: Blooms very early in spring; blossoms are easily killed by late frosts, so site in a frost-free, sheltered spot or cover/move container trees during flowering (RHS, Clemson, UMN).
Needs sufficient winter chill (hours at or below ~7 C / 45 F) to break dormancy and set fruit; choose a cultivar matched to local chill accumulation (Clemson, Almanac).
Self-fertile but flowers early when few pollinators are active; hand-pollinate container or sheltered trees to ensure a good crop (RHS).

Container minimum of ~70 L is a practical large-pot size for a dwarf/patio peach (RHS recommends a pot at least 45 cm wide and deep, ~71 L); full-size, standard peach trees are in-ground plants and will not reach full size or yield in a container.
Prone to root problems in waterlogged soil; plant in moist but well-drained soil and avoid overwatering.
Cold hardiness varies by cultivar; hardy types (e.g. Reliance, Contender) survive to about USDA zone 4 while many cultivars are best in zones 5-8.

source.citation: Clemson Cooperative Extension, Home & Garden Information Center — Peaches & Nectarines: full-sun siting, self-fruitful (only one variety needed), 10-10-10 plus calcium nitrate fertilizer schedule for young trees, dormant planting early December to mid-March, harvest mid-June to mid-September, chill requirement (hours at 45 F / 7 F or less).

Clemson Cooperative Extension — Peaches & Nectarines: fertilizer program for peaches (10-10-10 in March, supplemental calcium nitrate in May and July for young trees), establishing the ~monthly growing-season feeding cadence and preferred balanced NPK.
Clemson Cooperative Extension — Peaches & Nectarines: peach flowers are self-fruitful, so it is not necessary to plant more than one variety.

University of Minnesota Extension — Growing stone fruits in the home garden: young fruit trees require 1-2 inches of water per week from rain or irrigation; stone fruits need at least a half day of sun (more sun = more fruit); nitrogen is the main annual nutrient; bare-root trees planted in early spring while dormant; blossoms easily damaged by spring freezes.

University of Minnesota Extension — Growing stone fruits in the home garden: hardy peach cultivars (Reliance, Contender, Intrepid) can be grown in Minnesota's colder zones (down to about zone 4), establishing the conservative cold-hardy lower limit and strong frost sensitivity of blossoms.

Royal Horticultural Society (RHS) — How to grow peaches: container peaches need a pot at least 45 cm (18 in) wide and deep with loam-based compost (about 71 L), feed container trees with high-potassium liquid feed every fortnight in the growing season or granular general-purpose feed each spring; all peach varieties are self-fertile; protect/move flowering trees from frost and hand-pollinate.

Royal Horticultural Society (RHS) — How to grow peaches: container-grown trees need a steady supply of moisture throughout the growing season and newly planted trees need regular watering in dry spells for the first couple of years; supports the container-leach feeding adjustment and frost-protection / hand-pollination guidance.

The Old Farmer's Almanac — Peaches: Planting, Growing, and Harvesting Peach Trees: peaches grow in USDA zones 4-10 and do best in zones 6-8; trees are self-pollinating; chill hours are hours at or below 45 F (7 C) needed to break dormancy; early spring planting and summer harvest windows.

Confidence note — heatStressC (35 C) and the hotWeatherMultiplier (1.5x watering in hot weather) are conservative engine estimates. The authoritative extension sources read (Clemson, UMN, RHS, Almanac) emphasize cold/frost hardiness and a fixed 1-2 in/week watering baseline but do not state an explicit upper heat-stress temperature or a quantitative hot-weather watering multiplier for peach; these values are inferred from the general stone-fruit watering guidance and should be revisited with a heat-specific extension source.
